name: Intermittent Production-Only Incident with Incomplete Evidence
incident_description: Intermittent one-way audio on outbound calls. Callers can hear the customer, but the customer can't hear the caller. Happens on roughly 5% of calls. Cannot reproduce in staging. No pattern in time of day, customer, or agent. Started approximately 1 week ago but only noticed 2 days ago. No error messages.

services.name: nat-gateway
services.role: Network address translation for outbound RTP

services.name: load-balancer
services.role: Distributes SIP traffic across telephony-gateway instances

recent_changes.description: AWS replaced NAT Gateway instance during maintenance window (standard lifecycle event, no notification)
Updated SRTP encryption from AES-128 to AES-256 for compliance

call_quality_metrics.affected_direction: customer cannot hear caller (outbound RTP path)

sip_traces.affected_call: SDP negotiation succeeds, both parties send RTP, but customer's RTP never reaches caller's media-server
sip_traces.working_call: SDP negotiation succeeds, bidirectional RTP flows normally

network_analysis.tcpdump_on_affected_call: Customer's RTP packets arrive at NAT gateway but are NOT forwarded to media-server

incomplete_evidence.srtp_key_exchange_logs: NOT AVAILABLE (encrypted, no debug logging enabled)
incomplete_evidence.staging_reproduction: FAILED — staging uses different NAT gateway + different SRTP config

root_cause: Two changes interacted: (1) SRTP cipher was upgraded from AES-128 to AES-256 on March 20, increasing RTP packet size by 16 bytes. (2) AWS silently replaced the NAT Gateway on March 22 during maintenance. The new NAT Gateway has a slightly different conntrack behavior — it drops UDP packets that exceed a certain size when the conntrack entry was established with smaller packets. The SRTP upgrade made outbound RTP packets 16 bytes larger. For ~5% of calls, the NAT conntrack entry is established during SDP negotiation (with smaller STUN packets), and when the larger SRTP packets arrive, they exceed the conntrack's expected size and are silently dropped. This only affects the outbound direction (caller → customer) because inbound packets traverse a different NAT path. Cannot reproduce in staging because staging uses the OLD NAT Gateway instance AND the old SRTP cipher.

red_herrings.description: NAT gateway packet drop rate is 0.01% (normal)
red_herrings.why_misleading: The drops are real but tiny in aggregate. They're 100% of affected calls' outbound RTP, but only 0.01% of total NAT traffic.

red_herrings.description: SDP negotiation succeeds on all calls
red_herrings.why_misleading: SDP uses SIP (TCP/TLS), not affected by NAT UDP issue. Negotiation works; media doesn't.

red_herrings.description: SRTP upgrade was 9 days ago, incident noticed 2 days ago
red_herrings.why_misleading: SRTP alone didn't cause it. The NAT Gateway replacement on March 22 was the second factor. 5% failure rate took days to notice.

red_herrings.description: Customer can hear caller (one direction works)
red_herrings.why_misleading: Suggests caller's setup is wrong, but actually it's the NAT path asymmetry — inbound and outbound use different NAT routes.
